documentation:
# django-tastypie

```{image} https://readthedocs.org/projects/django-tastypie/badge/
:alt: Docs
:target: https://django-tastypie.readthedocs.io/
```

```{image} https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/actions/workflows/python-package.yml/badge.svg
:alt: CI
:target: https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/actions
```

```{image} https://coveralls.io/repos/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/badge.svg?service=github
:alt: Code Coverage
:target: https://coveralls.io/github/django-tastypie/django-tastypie
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/django-tastypie.svg
:alt: Version
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-tastypie
```

```{image} https://pypi-badges.global.ssl.fastly.net/svg?package=django-tastypie&timeframe=monthly
:alt: Downloads
:target: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-tastypie
```

Creating delicious APIs for Django apps since 2010.

Currently in beta but being used actively in production on several
sites.

## Requirements

### Core

- Python 3.8+ (Whatever is supported by your version of Django)
- Django 4.2 (LTS releases) or Django 4.0+ (intermediate releases, including 5.0 and 5.1)
- dateutil (<http://labix.org/python-dateutil>) >= 2.1

### Format Support

- XML: lxml 3 (<http://lxml.de/>) and defusedxml (<https://pypi.python.org/pypi/defusedxml>)
- YAML: pyyaml (<http://pyyaml.org/>)
- binary plist: biplist (<https://bitbucket.org/wooster/biplist>)

### Optional

- HTTP Digest authentication: python3-digest (<https://bitbucket.org/akoha/python-digest/>)

## What's It Look Like?

A basic example looks like:

```python
# myapp/api.py
# ============
from tastypie.resources import ModelResource
from myapp.models import Entry


class EntryResource(ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        queryset = Entry.objects.all()


# urls.py
# =======
from django.urls.conf import re_path, include
from tastypie.api import Api
from myapp.api import EntryResource

v1_api = Api(api_name='v1')
v1_api.register(EntryResource())

urlpatterns = [
    # The normal jazz here then...
    re_path(r'^api/', include(v1_api.urls)),
]
```

That gets you a fully working, read-write API for the `Entry` model that
supports all CRUD operations in a RESTful way. JSON/XML/YAML support is already
there, and it's easy to add related data/authentication/caching.

You can find more in the documentation at
<https://django-tastypie.readthedocs.io/>.

## Why Tastypie?

There are other API frameworks out there for Django. You need to
assess the options available and decide for yourself. That said, here are some
common reasons for tastypie.

- You need an API that is RESTful and uses HTTP well.
- You want to support deep relations.
- You DON'T want to have to write your own serializer to make the output right.
- You want an API framework that has little magic, very flexible and maps well to
  the problem domain.
- You want/need XML serialization that is treated equally to JSON (and YAML is
  there too).

## Reference Material

- <https://django-tastypie.readthedocs.io/en/latest/>
- <https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/tree/master/tests/basic> shows
  basic usage of tastypie
- <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/REST>
- <http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes>
- <http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2616.txt>
- <http://jacobian.org/writing/rest-worst-practices/>

## Getting Help

There are two primary ways of getting help.

1. Go to [StackOverflow] and post a question with the `tastypie` tag.
2. We have an IRC channel ([#tastypie on irc.freenode.net]) to get help,
   bounce an idea by us, or generally shoot the breeze.

## Security

Tastypie is committed to providing a flexible and secure API, and was designed
with many security features and options in mind. Due to the complex nature of
APIs and the constant discovery of new attack vectors and vulnerabilities,
no software is immune to security holes. We rely on our community to report
and help us investigate security issues.

If you come across a security hole **please do not open a Github issue**.
Instead, **drop us an email** at `tastypie-security@googlegroups.com`

We'll then work together to investigate and resolve the problem so we can
announce a solution along with the vulnerability.

[#tastypie on irc.freenode.net]: irc://irc.freenode.net/tastypie
[stackoverflow]: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/tastypie

classes.name: tastypie.bundle.Bundle
documentation:
A small container for instances and converted data for the
`dehydrate/hydrate` cycle.

Necessary because the `dehydrate/hydrate` cycle needs to access data at
different points.
In a separate file to avoid circular imports...

classes.name: tastypie.validation.Validation

methods.name: tastypie.validation.Validation.is_valid
documentation:
Performs a check on the data within the bundle (and optionally the
request) to ensure it is valid.

Should return a dictionary of error messages. If the dictionary has
zero items, the data is considered valid. If there are errors, keys
in the dictionary should be field names and the values should be a list
of errors, even if there is only one.
A basic validation stub that does no validation.

classes.name: tastypie.validation.FormValidation

methods.name: tastypie.validation.FormValidation.form_args
documentation:
Use the model data to generate the form arguments to be used for
validation.  In the case of fields that had to be hydrated (such as
FK relationships), be sure to use the hydrated value (comes from
model_to_dict()) rather than the value in bundle.data, since the latter
would likely not validate as the form won't expect a URI.

methods.name: tastypie.validation.FormValidation.is_valid
documentation:
Performs a check on bundle.data\`\`to ensure it is valid.

If the form is valid, an empty list (all valid) will be returned. If
not, a list of errors will be returned.

A validation class that uses a Django `Form` to validate the data.

This class **DOES NOT** alter the data sent, only verifies it. If you
want to alter the data, please use the `CleanedDataFormValidation` class
instead.

This class requires a `form_class` argument, which should be a Django
`Form` (or `ModelForm`, though `save` will never be called) class.
This form will be used to validate the data in `bundle.data`.

classes.name: tastypie.validation.CleanedDataFormValidation

methods.name: tastypie.validation.CleanedDataFormValidation.is_valid
documentation:
Checks bundle.data\`\`to ensure it is valid & replaces it with the
cleaned results.

If the form is valid, an empty list (all valid) will be returned. If
not, a list of errors will be returned.

A validation class that uses a Django `Form` to validate the data.

This class **ALTERS** data sent by the user!!!

This class requires a `form_class` argument, which should be a Django
`Form` (or `ModelForm`, though `save` will never be called) class.
This form will be used to validate the data in `bundle.data`.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.get_mime_for_format
documentation:
Given a format, attempts to determine the correct MIME type.

If not available on the current `Serializer`, returns
`application/json` by default.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.format_datetime
documentation:
A hook to control how datetimes are formatted.

Can be overridden at the `Serializer` level (`datetime_formatting`)
or globally (via `settings.TASTYPIE_DATETIME_FORMATTING`).

Default is `iso-8601`, which looks like "2010-12-16T03:02:14".

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.format_date
documentation:
A hook to control how dates are formatted.

Can be overridden at the `Serializer` level (`datetime_formatting`)
or globally (via `settings.TASTYPIE_DATETIME_FORMATTING`).

Default is `iso-8601`, which looks like "2010-12-16".

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.format_time
documentation:
A hook to control how times are formatted.

Can be overridden at the `Serializer` level (`datetime_formatting`)
or globally (via `settings.TASTYPIE_DATETIME_FORMATTING`).

Default is `iso-8601`, which looks like "03:02:14".

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.serialize

Given some data and a format, calls the correct method to serialize

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.deserialize
documentation:
Given some data and a format, calls the correct method to deserialize

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_simple

For a piece of data, attempts to recognize it and provide a simplified
form of something complex.

This brings complex Python data structures down to native types of the

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_etree
documentation:
Given some data, converts that data to an `etree.Element` suitable
for use in the XML output.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.from_etree
documentation:
Not the smartest deserializer on the planet. At the request level,
it first tries to output the deserialized subelement called "object"
or "objects" and falls back to deserializing based on hinted types in
the XML element attribute "type".

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_json
documentation: Given some Python data, produces JSON output.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.from_json
documentation: Given some JSON data, returns a Python dictionary of the decoded data.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_jsonp
documentation:
Given some Python data, produces JSON output wrapped in the provided
callback.

Due to a difference between JSON and Javascript, two
newline characters,   and  , need to be escaped.
See http://timelessrepo.com/json-isnt-a-javascript-subset for
details.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_xml
documentation: Given some Python data, produces XML output.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.from_xml
documentation:
Given some XML data, returns a Python dictionary of the decoded data.

By default XML entity declarations and DTDs will raise a BadRequest
exception content but subclasses may choose to override this if
necessary.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_yaml
documentation: Given some Python data, produces YAML output.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.from_yaml
documentation: Given some YAML data, returns a Python dictionary of the decoded data.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.to_plist
documentation: Given some Python data, produces binary plist output.

methods.name: tastypie.serializers.Serializer.from_plist
documentation:
Given some binary plist data, returns a Python dictionary of the
decoded data.

A swappable class for serialization.

This handles most types of data as well as the following output formats:

```
* json
* jsonp (Disabled by default)
* xml
* yaml
* plist (see https://bitbucket.org/wooster/biplist)
```

It was designed to make changing behavior easy, either by overridding the
various format methods (i.e. `to_json`), by changing the
`formats/content_types` options or by altering the other hook methods.

classes.name: tastypie.authorization.DjangoAuthorization

classes.name: tastypie.fields.ApiField

methods.name: tastypie.fields.ApiField.__init__
documentation:
Sets up the field. This is generally called when the containing
`Resource` is initialized.

Optionally accepts an `attribute`, which should be a string of
either an instance attribute or callable off the object during the
`dehydrate` or push data onto an object during the `hydrate`.
Defaults to `None`, meaning data will be manually accessed.

Optionally accepts a `default`, which provides default data when the
object being `dehydrated`/`hydrated` has no data on the field.
Defaults to `NOT_PROVIDED`.

Optionally accepts a `null`, which indicated whether or not a
`None` is allowable data on the field. Defaults to `False`.

Optionally accepts a `blank`, which indicated whether or not
data may be omitted on the field. Defaults to `False`.

Optionally accepts a `readonly`, which indicates whether the field
is used during the `hydrate` or not. Defaults to `False`.

Optionally accepts a `unique`, which indicates if the field is a
unique identifier for the object.

Optionally accepts `help_text`, which lets you provide a
human-readable description of the field exposed at the schema level.
Defaults to the per-Field definition.

Optionally accepts `use_in`. This may be one of `list`, `detail`
`all` or a callable which accepts a `bundle` and returns
`True` or `False`. Indicates wheather this field will be included
during dehydration of a list of objects or a single object. If `use_in`
is a callable, and returns `True`, the field will be included during
dehydration.
Defaults to `all`.

Optionally accepts `verbose_name`, which lets you provide a
more verbose name of the field exposed at the schema level.

classes.name: tastypie.fields.RelatedField

methods.name: tastypie.fields.RelatedField.__init__
documentation:
Builds the field and prepares it to access to related data.

The `to` argument should point to a `Resource` class, NOT
to a `Model`. Required.

The `attribute` argument should specify what field/callable points to
the related data on the instance object. Required.

Optionally accepts a `related_name` argument. Currently unused, as
unlike Django's ORM layer, reverse relations between `Resource`
classes are not automatically created. Defaults to `None`.

Optionally accepts a `null`, which indicated whether or not a
`None` is allowable data on the field. Defaults to `False`.

Optionally accepts a `blank`, which indicated whether or not
data may be omitted on the field. Defaults to `False`.

Optionally accepts a `readonly`, which indicates whether the field
is used during the `hydrate` or not. Defaults to `False`.

Optionally accepts a `full`, which indicates how the related
`Resource` will appear post-`dehydrate`. If `False`, the
related `Resource` will appear as a URL to the endpoint of that
resource. If `True`, the result of the sub-resource's
`dehydrate` will be included in full.

Optionally accepts a `unique`, which indicates if the field is a
unique identifier for the object.

Optionally accepts `help_text`, which lets you provide a
human-readable description of the field exposed at the schema level.
Defaults to the per-Field definition.

Optionally accepts `use_in`. This may be one of `list`, `detail`
`all` or a callable which accepts a `bundle` and returns
`True` or `False`. Indicates wheather this field will be included
during dehydration of a list of objects or a single object. If `use_in`
is a callable, and returns `True`, the field will be included during
dehydration.
Defaults to `all`.

Optionally accepts `verbose_name`, which lets you provide a
more verbose name of the field exposed at the schema level.

Optionally accepts a `full_list`, which indicated whether or not
data should be fully dehydrated when the request is for a list of
resources. Accepts `True`, `False` or a callable that accepts
a bundle and returns `True` or `False`. Depends on `full`
being `True`. Defaults to `True`.

Optionally accepts a `full_detail`, which indicated whether or not
data should be fully dehydrated when then request is for a single
resource. Accepts `True`, `False` or a callable that accepts a
bundle and returns `True` or `False`.Depends on `full`
being `True`. Defaults to `True`.

methods.name: tastypie.fields.RelatedField.build_related_resource
documentation:
Returns a bundle of data built by the related resource, usually via
`hydrate` with the data provided.

Accepts either a URI, a data dictionary (or dictionary-like structure)
or an object with a `pk`.

methods.name: tastypie.fields.RelatedField.should_full_dehydrate
documentation:
Based on the `full`, `list_full` and `detail_full` returns `True` or `False`
indicating weather the resource should be fully dehydrated.

Provides access to data that is related within the database.

The `RelatedField` base class is not intended for direct use but provides
functionality that `ToOneField` and `ToManyField` build upon.

The contents of this field actually point to another `Resource`,
rather than the related object. This allows the field to represent its data
in different ways.

The abstractions based around this are "leaky" in that, unlike the other
fields provided by `tastypie`, these fields don't handle arbitrary objects
very well. The subclasses use Django's ORM layer to make things go, though
there is no ORM-specific code at this level.

classes.name: tastypie.fields.ToOneField
documentation:
Provides access to related data via foreign key.

This subclass requires Django's ORM layer to work properly.

classes.name: tastypie.fields.ForeignKey
documentation: A convenience subclass for those who prefer to mirror `django.db.models`.

classes.name: tastypie.fields.OneToOneField

classes.name: tastypie.fields.ToManyField
documentation:
Provides access to related data via a join table.

This subclass requires Django's ORM layer to work properly.

Note that the `hydrate` portions of this field are quite different than
any other field. `hydrate_m2m` actually handles the data and relations.
This is due to the way Django implements M2M relationships.

methods.name: tastypie.test.TestApiClient.__init__

methods.name: tastypie.test.TestApiClient.get
documentation:
> Performs a simulated `GET` request to the provided URI.
>
> Optionally accepts a `data` kwarg, which in the case of `GET`, lets
> you send along `GET` parameters. This is useful when testing
> filtering or other things that read off the `GET` params. Example:
>
> ```
> from tastypie.test import TestApiClient
> client = TestApiClient()
>
> response = client.get('/api/v1/entry/1/', data={
>     'format': 'json',
>     'title__startswith': 'a',
>     'limit': 20,
>     'offset': 60
> })
> ```
>
> Optionally accepts an `authentication` kwarg, which should be an HTTP
> header with the correct authentication data already setup.
>
> All other `**kwargs` passed in get passed through to the Django
> `TestClient`. See

<https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/topics/testing/#module-django.test.client>

: for details.

methods.name: tastypie.test.TestApiClient.post

> the `data` gets serialized & sent as the body instead of becoming
> part of the URI.

>

> from tastypie.test import TestApiClient

> response = client.post('/api/v1/entry/', data={

>     'slug': 'another-post',
>     'title': 'Another Post',

A simplified, swappable base class for throttling.

Does nothing save for simulating the throttling API and implementing
some common bits for the subclasses.

Accepts a number of optional kwargs:

```
* ``throttle_at`` - the number of requests at which the user should
  be throttled. Default is 150 requests.
* ``timeframe`` - the length of time (in seconds) in which the user
  make up to the ``throttle_at`` requests. Default is 3600 seconds (
  1 hour).
* ``expiration`` - the length of time to retain the times the user
  has accessed the api in the cache. Default is 604800 (1 week).
```

classes.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.__init__
documentation:
Instantiates the `Paginator` and allows for some configuration.

The `request_data` argument ought to be a dictionary-like object.
May provide `limit` and/or `offset` to override the defaults.
Commonly provided `request.GET`. Required.

The `objects` should be a list-like object of `Resources`.
This is typically a `QuerySet` but can be anything that
implements slicing. Required.

Optionally accepts a `limit` argument, which specifies how many
items to show at a time. Defaults to `None`, which is no limit.

Optionally accepts an `offset` argument, which specifies where in
the `objects` to start displaying results from. Defaults to 0.

Optionally accepts a `max_limit` argument, which the upper bound
limit. Defaults to `1000`. If you set it to 0 or `None`, no upper
bound will be enforced.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.get_limit
documentation:
Determines the proper maximum number of results to return.

In order of importance, it will use:

> - The user-requested `limit` from the GET parameters, if specified.
> - The object-level `limit` if specified.
> - `settings.API_LIMIT_PER_PAGE` if specified.

Default is 20 per page.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.get_offset
documentation:
Determines the proper starting offset of results to return.

It attempts to use the user-provided `offset` from the GET parameters,
if specified. Otherwise, it falls back to the object-level `offset`.

Default is 0.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.get_slice
documentation: Slices the result set to the specified `limit` & `offset`.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.get_count
documentation: Returns a count of the total number of objects seen.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.get_previous
documentation:
If a previous page is available, will generate a URL to request that
page. If not available, this returns `None`.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.get_next
documentation:
If a next page is available, will generate a URL to request that
page. If not available, this returns `None`.

methods.name: tastypie.paginator.Paginator.page
documentation:
Generates all pertinent data about the requested page.

Handles getting the correct `limit` & `offset`, then slices off
the correct set of results and returns all pertinent metadata.

Limits result sets down to sane amounts for passing to the client.

This is used in place of Django's `Paginator` due to the way pagination
works. `limit` & `offset` (tastypie) are used in place of `page`
(Django) so none of the page-related calculations are necessary.

This implementation also provides additional details like the
`total_count` of resources seen and convenience links to the
`previous`/`next` pages of data as available.

functions.name: tastypie.utils.mime.determine_format
documentation:
Tries to "smartly" determine which output format is desired.

First attempts to find a `format` override from the request and supplies
that if found.

If no request format was demanded, it falls back to `mimeparse` and the
`Accepts` header, allowing specification that way.

If still no format is found, returns the `default_format` (which defaults
to `application/json` if not provided).

NOTE: callers *must* be prepared to handle BadRequest exceptions due to

: malformed HTTP request headers!

functions.name: tastypie.utils.validate_jsonp.is_valid_javascript_identifier
documentation: Return whether the given `id` is a valid Javascript identifier.
the core validation functions

functions.name: tastypie.utils.validate_jsonp.test
documentation:
The function `is_valid_javascript_identifier` validates a given
identifier according to the latest draft of the ECMAScript 5 Specification:

> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('hello')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('alert()')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('a-b')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('23foo')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('foo23')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('$210')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier(u'Straße')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier(r'b') # u'b'
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier(r' ')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('_bar')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('some_var')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_javascript_identifier('$')
> True
> ```

But `is_valid_jsonp_callback_value` is the function you want to use for
validating JSON-P callback parameter values:

> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('somevar')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('function')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value(' somevar')
> False
> ```

It supports the possibility of '.' being present in the callback name, e.g.

> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('$.ajaxHandler')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('$.23')
> False
> ```

As well as the pattern of providing an array index lookup, e.g.

> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('array_of_functions[42]')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('array_of_functions[42][1]')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('$.ajaxHandler[42][1].foo')
> True
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('array_of_functions[42]foo[1]')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('array_of_functions[]')
> False
> ```
>
> ```pycon
> >>> is_valid_jsonp_callback_value('array_of_functions["key"]')
> False
> ```

Enjoy!

documentation: Checks to make sure the user is logged in & has a Django session.

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.SessionAuthentication.get_identifier
documentation:
Provides a unique string identifier for the requestor.

This implementation returns the user's username.

An authentication mechanism that piggy-backs on Django sessions.

This is useful when the API is talking to Javascript on the same site.
Relies on the user being logged in through the standard Django login
setup.

Requires a valid CSRF token.

classes.name: tastypie.authentication.DigestAuthentication

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.DigestAuthentication.is_authenticated
documentation:
Finds the user and checks their API key.

Should return either `True` if allowed, `False` if not or an
`HttpResponse` if you need something custom.

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.DigestAuthentication.get_key
documentation:
Attempts to find the API key for the user. Uses `ApiKey` by default
but can be overridden.

Note that this behaves differently than the `ApiKeyAuthentication`
method of the same name.

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.DigestAuthentication.get_identifier
documentation:
Provides a unique string identifier for the requestor.

This implementation returns the user's username.

Handles HTTP Digest auth against a specific auth backend if provided,
or against all configured authentication backends using the
`authenticate` method from `django.contrib.auth`. However, instead of
the user's password, their API key should be used.

Optional keyword arguments:

`backend`

: If specified, use a specific `django.contrib.auth` backend instead
  of checking all backends specified in the `AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS`
  setting.

`realm`

: The realm to use in the `HttpUnauthorized` response.  Default:
  `django-tastypie`.

classes.name: tastypie.authentication.OAuthAuthentication

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.OAuthAuthentication.is_in
documentation:
Checks to ensure that all the OAuth parameter names are in the
provided `params`.

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.OAuthAuthentication.is_valid_request
documentation:
Checks whether the required parameters are either in the HTTP
`Authorization` header sent by some clients (the preferred method
according to OAuth spec) or fall back to `GET/POST`.

Handles OAuth, which checks a user's credentials against a separate service.
Currently verifies against OAuth 1.0a services.

This does *NOT* provide OAuth authentication in your API, strictly
consumption.

classes.name: tastypie.authentication.MultiAuthentication

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.MultiAuthentication.is_authenticated
documentation:
Identifies if the user is authenticated to continue or not.

Should return either `True` if allowed, `False` if not or an
`HttpResponse` if you need something custom.

methods.name: tastypie.authentication.MultiAuthentication.get_identifier
documentation:
Provides a unique string identifier for the requestor.

This implementation returns a combination of IP address and hostname.
An authentication backend that tries a number of backends in order.

functions.name: tastypie.resources.convert_post_to_VERB
documentation: Force Django to process the VERB.
Based off of ``piston.utils.coerce_put_post``. Similarly BSD-licensed.
And no, the irony is not lost on me.

classes.name: tastypie.resources.ResourceOptions
documentation:
A configuration class for `Resource`.

Provides sane defaults and the logic needed to augment these settings with
the internal `class Meta` used on `Resource` subclasses.

classes.name: tastypie.resources.Resource

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.wrap_view
documentation:
Wraps methods so they can be called in a more functional way as well
as handling exceptions better.

Note that if `BadRequest` or an exception with a `response` attr
are seen, there is special handling to either present a message back
to the user or return the response traveling with the exception.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_response_class_for_exception
documentation:
Can be overridden to customize response classes used for uncaught
exceptions. Should always return a subclass of
`django.http.HttpResponse`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource._build_reverse_url
documentation:
A convenience hook for overriding how URLs are built.

See `NamespacedModelResource._build_reverse_url` for an example.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.base_urls
documentation: The standard URLs this `Resource` should respond to.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.override_urls
documentation: Deprecated. Will be removed by v1.0.0. Please use `prepend_urls` instead.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.prepend_urls
documentation: A hook for adding your own URLs or matching before the default URLs.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.urls
documentation:
The endpoints this `Resource` responds to.

Mostly a standard URLconf, this is suitable for either automatic use
when registered with an `Api` class or for including directly in
a URLconf should you choose to.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.determine_format
documentation:
Used to determine the desired format.

Largely relies on `tastypie.utils.mime.determine_format` but here
as a point of extension.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.serialize
documentation:
Given a request, data and a desired format, produces a serialized
version suitable for transfer over the wire.

Mostly a hook, this uses the `Serializer` from `Resource._meta`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.deserialize
documentation:
Given a request, data and a format, deserializes the given data.

It relies on the request properly sending a `CONTENT_TYPE` header,
falling back to `application/json` if not provided.

Mostly a hook, this uses the `Serializer` from `Resource._meta`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.alter_list_data_to_serialize
documentation:
A hook to alter list data just before it gets serialized & sent to the user.

Useful for restructuring/renaming aspects of the what's going to be
sent.

Should accommodate for a list of objects, generally also including
meta data.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.alter_detail_data_to_serialize
documentation:
A hook to alter detail data just before it gets serialized & sent to the user.

Useful for restructuring/renaming aspects of the what's going to be
sent.

Should accommodate for receiving a single bundle of data.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.alter_deserialized_list_data
documentation:
A hook to alter list data just after it has been received from the user &
gets deserialized.

Useful for altering the user data before any hydration is applied.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.alter_deserialized_detail_data
documentation:
A hook to alter detail data just after it has been received from the user &
gets deserialized.

Useful for altering the user data before any hydration is applied.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.dispatch_list
documentation:
A view for handling the various HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) over
the entire list of resources.

Relies on `Resource.dispatch` for the heavy-lifting.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.dispatch_detail
documentation:
A view for handling the various HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) on
a single resource.

Relies on `Resource.dispatch` for the heavy-lifting.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.dispatch
documentation:
Handles the common operations (allowed HTTP method, authentication,
throttling, method lookup) surrounding most CRUD interactions.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.remove_api_resource_names
documentation:
Given a dictionary of regex matches from a URLconf, removes
`api_name` and/or `resource_name` if found.

This is useful for converting URLconf matches into something suitable
for data lookup. For example:

```
Model.objects.filter(**self.remove_api_resource_names(matches))
```

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.method_check
documentation:
Ensures that the HTTP method used on the request is allowed to be
handled by the resource.

Takes an `allowed` parameter, which should be a list of lowercase
HTTP methods to check against. Usually, this looks like:

```
# The most generic lookup.
self.method_check(request, self._meta.allowed_methods)

# A lookup against what's allowed for list-type methods.
self.method_check(request, self._meta.list_allowed_methods)

# A useful check when creating a new endpoint that only handles
# GET.
self.method_check(request, ['get'])
```

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.is_authenticated
documentation:
Handles checking if the user is authenticated and dealing with
unauthenticated users.

Mostly a hook, this uses class assigned to `authentication` from
`Resource._meta`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.throttle_check
documentation:
Handles checking if the user should be throttled.

Mostly a hook, this uses class assigned to `throttle` from
`Resource._meta`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.log_throttled_access
documentation:
Handles the recording of the user's access for throttling purposes.

Mostly a hook, this uses class assigned to `throttle` from
`Resource._meta`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_read_list
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to GET this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_read_detail
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to GET this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_create_list
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to POST this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_create_detail
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to POST this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_update_list
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to PUT this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_update_detail
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to PUT this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_delete_list
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to DELETE this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.authorized_delete_detail
documentation:
Handles checking of permissions to see if the user has authorization
to DELETE this resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.build_bundle
documentation:
Given either an object, a data dictionary or both, builds a `Bundle`
for use throughout the `dehydrate/hydrate` cycle.

If no object is provided, an empty object from
`Resource._meta.object_class` is created so that attempts to access
`bundle.obj` do not fail.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.build_filters
documentation:
Allows for the filtering of applicable objects.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.'

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.apply_sorting
documentation:
Allows for the sorting of objects being returned.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_bundle_detail_data
documentation:
Convenience method to return the `detail_uri_name` attribute off
`bundle.obj`.

Usually just accesses `bundle.obj.pk` by default.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.detail_uri_kwargs
documentation:
Given a `Bundle` or an object (typically a `Model` instance),
it returns the extra kwargs needed to generate a detail URI.

By default, it uses this resource's `detail_uri_name` in order to
create the URI.
URL-related methods.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.resource_uri_kwargs
documentation:
Builds a dictionary of kwargs to help generate URIs.

Automatically provides the `Resource.Meta.resource_name` (and
optionally the `Resource.Meta.api_name` if populated by an `Api`
object).

If the `bundle_or_obj` argument is provided, it calls
`Resource.detail_uri_kwargs` for additional bits to create

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_resource_uri
documentation:
Handles generating a resource URI.

If the `bundle_or_obj` argument is not provided, it builds the URI
for the list endpoint.

If the `bundle_or_obj` argument is provided, it builds the URI for
the detail endpoint.

Return the generated URI. If that URI can not be reversed (not found
in the URLconf), it will return an empty string.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_via_uri
documentation:
This pulls apart the salient bits of the URI and populates the
resource via a `obj_get`.

Optionally accepts a `request`.

If you need custom behavior based on other portions of the URI,
simply override this method.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.full_dehydrate
documentation:
Given a bundle with an object instance, extract the information from it
to populate the resource.
Data preparation.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.dehydrate
documentation:
A hook to allow a final manipulation of data once all fields/methods
have built out the dehydrated data.

Useful if you need to access more than one dehydrated field or want
to annotate on additional data.

Must return the modified bundle.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.full_hydrate
documentation:
Given a populated bundle, distill it and turn it back into
a full-fledged object instance.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.hydrate
documentation:
A hook to allow an initial manipulation of data before all methods/fields
have built out the hydrated data.

Useful if you need to access more than one hydrated field or want
to annotate on additional data.

Must return the modified bundle.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.hydrate_m2m
documentation: Populate the ManyToMany data on the instance.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.build_schema
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of all the fields on the resource and some
properties about those fields.

Used by the `schema/` endpoint to describe what will be available.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.dehydrate_resource_uri
documentation:
For the automatically included `resource_uri` field, dehydrate
the URI for the given bundle.

Returns empty string if no URI can be generated.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.generate_cache_key
documentation:
Creates a unique-enough cache key.

This is based off the current api_name/resource_name/args/kwargs.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_object_list
documentation:
A hook to allow making returning the list of available objects.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.
Data access methods.

documentation: Checks to ensure `post` is within `allowed_methods`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.can_update

Checks to ensure `put` is within `allowed_methods`.

Used when hydrating related data.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.can_delete
documentation: Checks to ensure `delete` is within `allowed_methods`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.apply_filters
documentation:
A hook to alter how the filters are applied to the object list.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's

Fetches the list of objects available on the resource.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.cached_obj_get_list

A version of `obj_get_list` that uses the cache as a means to get
commonly-accessed data faster.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.obj_get

Fetches an individual object on the resource.

This needs to be implemented at the user level. If the object can not
be found, this should raise a `NotFound` exception.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.cached_obj_get
documentation:
A version of `obj_get` that uses the cache as a means to get
commonly-accessed data faster.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.obj_create

Creates a new object based on the provided data.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.obj_update
documentation:
Updates an existing object (or creates a new object) based on the
provided data.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

documentation:
Deletes an entire list of objects.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.obj_delete_list_for_update
documentation:
Deletes an entire list of objects, specific to PUT list.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.obj_delete
documentation:
Deletes a single object.

This needs to be implemented at the user level.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.create_response
documentation:
Extracts the common "which-format/serialize/return-response" cycle.

Mostly a useful shortcut/hook.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.error_response
documentation:
Extracts the common "which-format/serialize/return-error-response"
cycle.

Should be used as much as possible to return errors.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.is_valid
documentation:
Handles checking if the data provided by the user is valid.

Mostly a hook, this uses class assigned to `validation` from
`Resource._meta`.

If validation fails, an error is raised with the error messages
serialized inside it.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.rollback
documentation:
Given the list of bundles, delete all objects pertaining to those
bundles.

This needs to be implemented at the user level. No exceptions should
be raised if possible.

`ModelResource` includes a full working version specific to Django's
`Models`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_list
documentation:
Returns a serialized list of resources.

Calls `obj_get_list` to provide the data, then handles that result
set and serializes it.

Should return a HttpResponse (200 OK).
Views.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_detail
documentation:
Returns a single serialized resource.

Calls `cached_obj_get/obj_get` to provide the data, then handles that result
set and serializes it.

Should return a HttpResponse (200 OK).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.post_list
documentation:
Creates a new resource/object with the provided data.

Calls `obj_create` with the provided data and returns a response
with the new resource's location.

If a new resource is created, return `HttpCreated` (201 Created).
If `Meta.always_return_data = True`, there will be a populated body
of serialized data.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.post_detail
documentation:
Creates a new subcollection of the resource under a resource.

This is not implemented by default because most people's data models
aren't self-referential.

If a new resource is created, return `HttpCreated` (201 Created).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.put_list
documentation:
Replaces a collection of resources with another collection.

Calls `delete_list` to clear out the collection then `obj_create`
with the provided the data to create the new collection.

Return `HttpNoContent` (204 No Content) if
`Meta.always_return_data = False` (default).

Return `HttpResponse` (200 OK) with new data if
`Meta.always_return_data = True`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.put_detail
documentation:
Either updates an existing resource or creates a new one with the
provided data.

Calls `obj_update` with the provided data first, but falls back to
`obj_create` if the object does not already exist.

If a new resource is created, return `HttpCreated` (201 Created).
If `Meta.always_return_data = True`, there will be a populated body
of serialized data.

If an existing resource is modified and
`Meta.always_return_data = False` (default), return `HttpNoContent`
(204 No Content).
If an existing resource is modified and
`Meta.always_return_data = True`, return `HttpAccepted` (200
OK).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.delete_list
documentation:
Destroys a collection of resources/objects.

Calls `obj_delete_list`.

If the resources are deleted, return `HttpNoContent` (204 No Content).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.delete_detail
documentation:
Destroys a single resource/object.

Calls `obj_delete`.

If the resource is deleted, return `HttpNoContent` (204 No Content).
If the resource did not exist, return `Http404` (404 Not Found).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.patch_list
documentation:
Updates a collection in-place.

The exact behavior of `PATCH` to a list resource is still the matter of
some debate in REST circles, and the `PATCH` RFC isn't standard. So the
behavior this method implements (described below) is something of a
stab in the dark. It's mostly cribbed from GData, with a smattering
of ActiveResource-isms and maybe even an original idea or two.

The `PATCH` format is one that's similar to the response returned from
a `GET` on a list resource:

```
{
  "objects": [{object}, {object}, ...],
  "deleted_objects": ["URI", "URI", "URI", ...],
}
```

For each object in `objects`:

> - If the dict does not have a `resource_uri` key then the item is
>   considered "new" and is handled like a `POST` to the resource list.
> - If the dict has a `resource_uri` key and the `resource_uri` refers
>   to an existing resource then the item is a update; it's treated
>   like a `PATCH` to the corresponding resource detail.
> - If the dict has a `resource_uri` but the resource *doesn't* exist,
>   then this is considered to be a create-via-`PUT`.

Each entry in `deleted_objects` referes to a resource URI of an existing
resource to be deleted; each is handled like a `DELETE` to the relevent
resource.

In any case:

> > - If there's a resource URI it *must* refer to a resource of this
> >   type. It's an error to include a URI of a different resource.
> > - `PATCH` is all or nothing. If a single sub-operation fails, the
> >   entire request will fail and all resources will be rolled back.
>
> - For `PATCH` to work, you **must** have `put` in your
>   {ref}`detail-allowed-methods` setting.
> - To delete objects via `deleted_objects` in a `PATCH` request you
>   **must** have `delete` in your {ref}`detail-allowed-methods`
>   setting.

Substitute appropriate names for `objects` and
`deleted_objects` if `Meta.collection_name` is set to something
other than `objects` (default).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.patch_detail
documentation:
Updates a resource in-place.

Calls `obj_update`.

If the resource is updated, return `HttpAccepted` (202 Accepted).
If the resource did not exist, return `HttpNotFound` (404 Not Found).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.update_in_place
documentation: Update the object in original_bundle in-place using new_data.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_schema
documentation:
Returns a serialized form of the schema of the resource.

Calls `build_schema` to generate the data. This method only responds
to HTTP GET.

Should return a HttpResponse (200 OK).

methods.name: tastypie.resources.Resource.get_multiple
documentation:
Returns a serialized list of resources based on the identifiers
from the URL.

Calls `obj_get_list` to fetch only the objects requests in
a single query. This method only responds to HTTP GET.

For backward compatibility the method `obj_get` is used if
`obj_get_list` is not implemented.

Should return a HttpResponse (200 OK).

Handles the data, request dispatch and responding to requests.

Serialization/deserialization is handled "at the edges" (i.e. at the
beginning/end of the request/response cycle) so that everything internally
is Python data structures.

This class tries to be non-model specific, so it can be hooked up to other
data sources, such as search results, files, other data, etc.

classes.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.should_skip_field
documentation:
Given a Django model field, return if it should be included in the
contributed ApiFields.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.api_field_from_django_field
documentation:
Returns the field type that would likely be associated with each
Django type.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.get_fields
documentation:
Given any explicit fields to include and fields to exclude, add
additional fields based on the associated model.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.check_filtering
documentation:
Given a field name, a optional filter type and an optional list of
additional relations, determine if a field can be filtered on.

If a filter does not meet the needed conditions, it should raise an
`InvalidFilterError`.

If the filter meets the conditions, a list of attribute names (not
field names) will be returned.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.filter_value_to_python
documentation: Turn the string `value` into a python object.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.build_filters
documentation:
Given a dictionary of filters, create the necessary ORM-level filters.

Keys should be resource fields, **NOT** model fields.

Valid values are either a list of Django filter types (i.e.
`['startswith', 'exact', 'lte']`), the `ALL` constant or the
`ALL_WITH_RELATIONS` constant.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.apply_sorting
documentation:
Given a dictionary of options, apply some ORM-level sorting to the
provided `QuerySet`.

Looks for the `order_by` key and handles either ascending (just the
field name) or descending (the field name with a `-` in front).

The field name should be the resource field, **NOT** model field.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.apply_filters
documentation:
An ORM-specific implementation of `apply_filters`.

The default simply applies the `applicable_filters` as `**kwargs`,
but should make it possible to do more advanced things.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.get_object_list
documentation:
An ORM-specific implementation of `get_object_list`.

Returns a queryset that may have been limited by other overrides.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_get_list
documentation:
A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_get_list`.

`GET` dictionary of bundle.request can be used to narrow the query.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_get
documentation:
A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_get`.

Takes optional `kwargs`, which are used to narrow the query to find
the instance.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_create
documentation: A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_create`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.lookup_kwargs_with_identifiers
documentation:
Kwargs here represent uri identifiers Ex: /repos/<user_id>/<repo_name>/
We need to turn those identifiers into Python objects for generating
lookup parameters that can find them in the DB

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_update
documentation: A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_update`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_delete_list
documentation: A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_delete_list`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_delete_list_for_update
documentation: A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_delete_list_for_update`.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.obj_delete
documentation:
A ORM-specific implementation of `obj_delete`.

Takes optional `kwargs`, which are used to narrow the query to find
the instance.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.patch_list
documentation:
An ORM-specific implementation of `patch_list`.

Necessary because PATCH should be atomic (all-success or all-fail)
and the only way to do this neatly is at the database level.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.rollback
documentation:
A ORM-specific implementation of `rollback`.

Given the list of bundles, delete all models pertaining to those
bundles.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.save_related
documentation:
Handles the saving of related non-M2M data.

Calling assigning `child.parent = parent` & then calling
`Child.save` isn't good enough to make sure the `parent`
is saved.

To get around this, we go through all our related fields &
call `save` on them if they have related, non-M2M data.
M2M data is handled by the `ModelResource.save_m2m` method.

methods.name: tastypie.resources.BaseModelResource.save_m2m
documentation:
Handles the saving of related M2M data.

Due to the way Django works, the M2M data must be handled after the
main instance, which is why this isn't a part of the main `save` bits.

Currently slightly inefficient in that it will clear out the whole
relation and recreate the related data as needed.

A subclass of `Resource` designed to work with Django's `Models`.

This class will introspect a given `Model` and build a field list based
on the fields found on the model (excluding relational fields).

Given that it is aware of Django's ORM, it also handles the CRUD data
operations of the resource.

classes.name: tastypie.resources.NamespacedModelResource
documentation: A ModelResource subclass that respects Django namespaces.
